comments:
Summary:
This paper proposes utterance-level Permutation Invariant Training (uPIT) for speaker-independent multi-talker speech separation. uPIT extends prior frame-level PIT by using an utterance-level permutation criterion combined with LSTM/BLSTM networks, removing the need for an explicit speaker-tracing or clustering step at inference. The authors evaluate uPIT extensively on WSJ0 (2- and 3-mix) and a Danish 2-mix dataset, report SDR and PESQ improvements, and compare against NMF, CASA, Deep Clustering (DPCL) variants, and Deep Attractor Networks (DANet). They show good generalization to unseen speakers and languages and demonstrate a single model can handle both two- and three-speaker mixtures.

Strengths:
- Addresses a key practical barrier (label permutation + frame-level tracing) in multi-speaker separation with a simple, elegant modification to the PIT objective. The method avoids clustering/attractor estimation at inference, which reduces complexity.
- Thorough experimental evaluation on multiple datasets, including open/closed speaker conditions and cross-language testing (English→Danish). Reported gains are substantial and competitive with state-of-the-art methods of the time.
- Careful ablations: compares different mask targets (AM, PSM, nonnegative PSM), model types (DNN, CNN, LSTM/BLSTM), training regimes (two-stage, dropout variants), and reports oracle comparisons (IRM/IPSM) to bound performance.
- Demonstrates that a single model can handle mixtures with different numbers of speakers by using a silent-channel trick — practically useful.

Weaknesses / Points for improvement:
- The method still reconstructs waveforms using the mixture phase; the manuscript notes phase estimation is open. While PSMs partly compensate, limitations of using mixture phase (and resulting perceptual quality limits) could be discussed and analyzed more thoroughly.
- Although comparisons to DPCL/DANet are provided, those methods sometimes used additional engineering (curriculum training, recurrent dropout). More discussion about matching experimental conditions (model size, training recipes) would help clarify fairness of comparisons.
- Real-world robustness is not evaluated: experiments are on simulated anechoic mixtures at 0–5 dB SNR. Performance under reverberation, real background noise, mismatched SNRs, or more than three speakers is not reported. Also, runtime/memory/latency and feasibility for real-time systems are not discussed.
- Same-gender separations remain noticeably harder; while reported, the paper could provide more analysis of failure modes (when and why the model fails) or strategies to mitigate them.
- Reproducibility: many implementation/training details are given (toolkit, architectures, dropout rates) but code or trained models are not referenced in the provided text. Public release would enhance impact.

Recommendation and suggestions:
Overall, this is a clear, well-motivated, and technically sound contribution with strong empirical support. It materially advances multi-talker separation by making a practical, end-to-end approach that sidesteps expensive inference-time clustering. I recommend acceptance and oral presentation. For revision or future work the authors should (a) include or cite public code/models if possible to aid reproducibility, (b) analyze robustness to reverberation and noise and consider complex-domain / phase-aware extensions, (c) expand discussion on computational costs and real-time applicability, and (d) provide deeper analysis of same-gender failure cases and potential mitigations.
